section.title: Released from the Law
section.sectionRationale: Having shown that believers have died to sin and been freed to serve God, Paul now turns to their relationship to the law. Using the analogy of marriage, he argues that the law binds a person only as long as he lives. As a widow is released from the marriage law when her husband dies, so believers, having died to the law through the body of Christ, are released from it to belong to another, to the risen Christ, so as to bear fruit to God and serve in the new way of the Spirit rather than the old way of the written code.
section.thesis: The law holds authority over a person only during life; and because believers have died to the law through the body of Christ, they are released from it and joined to the risen Christ, so that they bear fruit to God and serve in the new way of the Spirit, not the old way of the letter.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the principle, the law binds only during life; the marriage analogy, a widow released to marry another; and the application, you died to the law through Christ to belong to the risen One and serve in the new way of the Spirit.

units.label: The law binds only during life

verses.text: Do you not know, brothers (for I am speaking to those who know the law), that the {{nomos|law}} has authority over a man only as long as he lives?

units.label: The marriage analogy
units.note: a widow released to marry another

verses.text: For instance, a married woman is bound by law to her husband as long as he lives. But if her husband dies, she is released from the law of marriage.

So then, if she is joined to another man while her husband is still alive, she is called an adulteress; but if her husband dies, she is free from that law and is not an adulteress if she marries another man.

units.label: Released to belong to another
units.note: died to the law to serve in the new way of the Spirit

verses.text: Therefore, my brothers, you also died to the law through the body of Christ, that you might belong to another, to Him who was raised from the dead, in order that we might bear fruit to God.
For when we lived according to the {{sarx|flesh}}, the sinful passions aroused by the law were at work in our bodies, bearing fruit for death.
But now, having died to what bound us, we have been released from the law, so that we serve in the new way of the {{pneuma|Spirit}}, and not in the old way of the {{gramma|written code}}.

nomos.gloss: Law, especially the law of Moses with its commands. Paul argues that the law exercises its binding authority over a person only during his lifetime. It is holy and good, but it cannot give life or free from sin; the believer's relationship to it is fundamentally changed through death with Christ, so that it no longer stands as the regime under which they live.

sarx.gloss: Flesh, here not merely the body but human nature in its weakness and hostility to God, the old sphere of existence apart from the Spirit. When we lived in the flesh, the law, though good, only stirred up sinful passions that bore fruit for death. The flesh is the realm from which believers have been rescued into the new life of the Spirit.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, the Holy Spirit, who marks the new age and the new way of serving God. Believers now serve in the new way of the Spirit, a service that flows from inward life and power rather than mere external command. The Spirit is the great gift of the new covenant, writing God's will on the heart and enabling obedience from within.

gramma.gloss: The written code or letter, the law as external written command. The old way of the letter could direct and condemn but could not empower obedience or give life. Paul contrasts it with the new way of the Spirit: not that the law was bad, but that the letter without the Spirit only exposed and provoked sin. The new covenant supplies what the letter could not.

basic.context: Paul has shown that believers died to sin and were freed to serve God. Now he asks about the law. Speaking to people who know the law, he reminds them that a law binds a person only while he is alive. Death ends its claim. He illustrates with marriage: a wife is bound to her husband until he dies, and then she is free to marry another.
basic.hermeneutics: The marriage picture is an analogy, not an allegory to be pressed at every point. Its single point is that death dissolves the binding of the law. Then Paul applies it: believers have died, through the body of Christ, so they are released from the law to belong to the risen Christ. Read verse 4 as the heart of the passage: a change of husbands, from the law to Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage turns on nomos, the law, which binds only during life. When we lived in the sarx, the flesh, the law stirred up sinful passions bearing fruit for death. But now we serve in the new way of the pneuma, the Spirit, not the old way of the gramma, the written code, the letter that could command but not give life.
basic.typology: The marriage bond points to the deeper union between Christ and His people, the true bridegroom to whom the church is now joined. The old covenant, given in written commands, gives way to the new covenant of the Spirit, which the prophets foretold, when God would write His law on the heart and put His Spirit within His people.
basic.christ: Believers are released from the law through the body of Christ, that is, through His crucified body offered in death. They now belong to another, to Him who was raised from the dead. Christ is the new husband to whom the church is joined, the risen Lord in whose life they now bear fruit to God. Union with Him, not the law, is the source of their new obedience.
basic.god: The whole aim of this release is that we might bear fruit to God. God is the one served in the new way of the Spirit, and the fruit of the new marriage to Christ is fruit for Him. God has provided in Christ and by the Spirit what the law could never supply, life and the power to obey from the heart.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 6 grounds this in dying and rising with Christ. Galatians 2 says through the law I died to the law so that I might live to God. 2 Corinthians 3 contrasts the letter that kills with the Spirit that gives life. Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant written on the heart.
basic.oneStory: The Bible moves from the law given at Sinai on tablets of stone to the new covenant promised by the prophets, when God would put His Spirit within His people. Here that turning is announced: believers, having died with Christ, are released from the old regime of the written law and joined to the risen Christ, serving God in the new way of the Spirit, and bearing fruit for Him.

context.literary: Chapter 7 continues the argument of chapter 6 about the believer's freedom, now focused on the law. Verse 1 states a principle, verses 2 and 3 give the marriage analogy, and verse 4 applies it, followed by a contrast between the old life in the flesh under the law and the new life in the Spirit. Verse 5 anticipates the extended treatment of law and sin that follows in verses 7 to 25.
context.historical: Paul addresses those who know the law, likely both Jewish believers and Gentiles familiar with the Scriptures. Roman and Jewish law alike recognized that death dissolved the marriage bond, so the analogy would be readily understood. The question of the law's ongoing role was pressing for a church of mixed background wrestling with how Jews and Gentiles relate to the Mosaic law in Christ.
context.canonical: This passage belongs to the New Testament teaching that believers are not under the law as a covenant regime but united to Christ under the new covenant of the Spirit. It stands with 2 Corinthians 3 and Galatians 2 to 5, and looks back to the promise of Jeremiah 31, showing the continuity and the newness of God's saving work.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that believers, having died with Christ, are released from the binding authority of the law and joined instead to the risen Christ, so that their obedience now springs from union with Him and the Spirit and bears fruit to God, rather than from the external written code that could only expose and provoke sin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes a change that has already occurred, believers have died to the law and been joined to Christ, and draws out its purpose: that we might bear fruit to God and serve in the new way of the Spirit. The abiding truth is that the believer's obedience flows from union with Christ, not from law as a covenant of acceptance.

debates.question: How far should the marriage analogy be pressed?

views.label: It makes one main point, that death dissolves the law's binding
views.case: The historic reading takes the analogy as illustrating a single truth: as death frees a widow to remarry, so death with Christ frees the believer from the law to belong to another; the details are not to be allegorized.

views.label: Every element must correspond precisely
views.case: Some try to match each figure exactly, asking who dies and who remarries; but Paul mixes the roles, since in verse 4 the believers themselves die, showing the analogy is meant to teach one truth, not a strict allegory.

debates.bearing: Reading it for its single point keeps the focus where Paul places it: through death with Christ the believer is lawfully released from the old regime and freely joined to the risen Lord to bear fruit to God.

debates.question: Does released from the law mean the law has no place at all for believers?

views.label: Freedom from the law as a covenant regime, not from all moral instruction
views.case: The historic Reformed reading holds that believers are freed from the law as a covenant of works and condemnation, yet the law still instructs and guides the Christian life, now written on the heart by the Spirit.

views.label: Complete abolition of the law in every sense
views.case: Some read it as the total abrogation of the law; but Paul immediately insists the law is holy, righteous, and good, so the release is from its condemning and provoking role, not from its abiding revelation of God's will.

debates.bearing: Distinguishing the law as covenant regime from the law as revelation of God's will guards both grace and holiness: the believer is free from condemnation yet serves God's good will in the power of the Spirit.

study.originalLanguages: The argument rests on nomos, the law, whose binding authority reaches only as far as a person's life; death ends its claim. Paul recalls the old existence in the sarx, the flesh, human nature apart from the Spirit, in which the law, though good, only stirred up sinful passions that bore fruit for death. Over against this stands the new way of the pneuma, the Spirit, the great gift of the new covenant who enables obedience from within, set against the gramma, the written code or letter, which could command and condemn but never give the life it required.

typology.fulfillment: The union of husband and wife points to Christ and His church; the believer, released from the law, is joined to Christ the risen bridegroom.

typology.type: The old covenant of the letter
typology.fulfillment: The law written on tablets of stone gives way to the new covenant of the Spirit, foretold by the prophets, written on the heart.
The barren fruit for death under the flesh is answered by the fruitfulness of the new union with the risen Christ, fulfilling the vine bearing fruit for God.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center of the release. Believers died to the law through the body of Christ, His crucified body offered in death, and are now joined to another, to Him who was raised from the dead. He is the risen bridegroom to whom the church is wedded, and it is in union with His risen life that believers now bear fruit to God. The whole passage moves from the old bond to a new marriage, and the new husband is the living Christ, so that Christian obedience is not law keeping for acceptance but the fruitfulness of belonging to Him.
study.god: The goal of the whole passage is that we might bear fruit to God. God is the one served, and the fruit of the new union with Christ is fruit for Him. He has provided by the Spirit what the written law could never supply: inward life and power to obey from the heart. The passage exalts a God who does not merely command righteousness from without but works it from within through His Spirit, gathering a fruitful people for Himself in Christ.

crossReferences.note: Believers died and rose with Christ, the death that grounds their release from the law.
Through the law I died to the law so that I might live to God, crucified with Christ.
The letter kills, but the Spirit gives life, the old written code and the new way of the Spirit.
The promise of a new covenant with God's law written on the heart, fulfilled in the Spirit.

study.oneStory: The Bible moves from Sinai, where the law was given on tablets of stone, to the prophets' promise of a new covenant when God would put His Spirit within His people and write His law on their hearts. Here Paul announces that turning point. Believers, having died with Christ, are released from the old regime of the written law and joined to the risen Christ as a bride to her husband, serving God in the new way of the Spirit and bearing fruit for Him.

quiz.q: What principle does Paul state about the law in verse 1?

options.text: The law has authority over a person only as long as he lives.
options.note: Death ends the law's binding claim, the key to the argument.

options.text: The law has authority over a person forever, even after death.
options.note: Paul says the opposite; death dissolves the law's authority.

options.text: The law never had any real authority at all.
options.note: The law is holy and had genuine authority; its claim simply ends at death.

options.text: The law binds only those who choose to accept it.
options.note: It rightly senses the law's claim is not permanent, but it is death, not personal choice, that ends the law's authority.

quiz.q: What is the single point of the marriage analogy?

options.text: Death dissolves the binding of the law, freeing one to belong to another.
options.note: As a widow is free to remarry, so death with Christ frees the believer.

options.text: That remarriage is always sinful.
options.note: Paul says the widow may remarry without sin; that is the point of freedom.

options.text: That every detail of marriage must match the law exactly.
options.note: The analogy does concern the law, but it makes one main point and is not a strict allegory to be pressed at every detail.

options.text: That the law is like a cruel husband to be escaped.
options.note: The law is holy; the point is release through death, not that the law is cruel.

quiz.q: How were believers released from the law, according to verse 4?

options.text: They died to the law through the body of Christ.
options.note: Through Christ's crucified body they died and were freed.

options.text: They simply decided the law no longer applied to them.
options.note: Believers are indeed free from the law, but by dying with Christ, not by their own decision that it no longer applies.

options.text: They kept the law perfectly and so graduated from it.
options.note: No one keeps the law perfectly; release is through Christ's death.

options.text: The law was abolished so that righteousness no longer matters.
options.note: The aim is to bear fruit to God, not to abandon righteousness.

quiz.q: To whom are believers joined after being released from the law?

options.text: To Christ, who was raised from the dead, so as to bear fruit to God.
options.note: The believer belongs to the risen Christ, the new bridegroom.

options.text: To no one; they now belong only to themselves.
options.note: They are joined to Christ, not left autonomous.

options.text: To a stricter version of the law.
options.note: They are joined to the risen Christ, not to a new law regime.

options.text: To the Spirit as a replacement for Christ.
options.note: Believers do serve in the new way of the Spirit, but they are joined to the risen Christ Himself, not to the Spirit as a replacement for Him.

quiz.q: What is the new way in which believers now serve God?

options.text: In the new way of the Spirit, not the old way of the written code.
options.note: The Spirit gives inward life the letter could not supply.

options.text: In the old way of the written code, kept more carefully.
options.note: This still values God's commands, but Paul contrasts the new way of the Spirit with the old way of the letter, not merely a more careful letter keeping.

options.text: By ignoring God's will altogether.
options.note: The new way bears fruit to God; it does not ignore His will.

options.text: By a private spirituality with no reference to God.
options.note: Serving in the Spirit is service to God, bearing fruit for Him.

questions: Paul says the law binds a person only during life, and believers have died with Christ. How does knowing you are released from the law as a way of acceptance change how you relate to God?
The believer is joined to the risen Christ as a bride to a husband. What does it mean for your daily life to belong to Christ rather than to the law?
Under the flesh, even the good law only stirred up sin and bore fruit for death. Where have you experienced that mere rules cannot change the heart?
The purpose of our release is to bear fruit to God. What kind of fruit is God looking for from your union with Christ?
We now serve in the new way of the Spirit, not the old way of the letter. What would it look like this week to obey from the Spirit's inward power rather than external duty alone?

translationNotes.note: Speaking to those who know the law addresses readers familiar with the Scriptures, both Jewish believers and Gentiles who knew them; the principle that law binds only during life was common to both Jewish and Roman legal thought.
Through the body of Christ refers to His crucified body offered in death; the believer's death with Christ is the ground of release from the law and of the new belonging to the risen Lord.

The old way of the written code renders the letter, gramma, the external written law; the contrast with the Spirit is not that the law was evil but that the letter could command and condemn while only the Spirit gives life.
